retrieved_docs.title: Third-Generation Cephalosporins -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Cephalosporins are contraindicated in patients with a known allergy to the cephalosporin group of antibiotics. Nevertheless, an allergy to a cephalosporin in one class may not induce any cross-reactivity to another agent within the same or different class/generation. [41] Moreover, cephalosporins share molecular similarities with penicillins and could lead to allergic reactions in 10% of patients with known allergies to penicillins. Nevertheless, the cross-reactivity between penicillins and cephalosporins is still debated and remains an open issue. [42] Cephalosporins, along with penicillins, are also contraindicated in patients who have a history of severe anaphylactic reactions with these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: Cephalosporins -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: One of the contraindications of cephalosporins is if patients are allergic to them or have had an anaphylactic reaction to penicillin or other β-lactam antimicrobials. The cross-reactivity is thought to be due to similar side chains, not the β-lactam ring. However, it is important to understand the contraindications according to the latest guidelines. The guidelines provided by the American Academy of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology (AAAAI) and the American College of Allergy, Asthma, and Immunology (ACAAI) offer recommendations for managing patients with a history of cephalosporin or penicillin allergies. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Cefepime -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Cephalosporins are contraindicated in patients with severe hypersensitivity reactions to penicillin. Early testing of cephalosporins (until the 1980s) came from the Penicillium mold of penicillin. The hypothesis is that these early cephalosporins were contaminated with penicillin and accounted for cross-reactivity allergic response. Thus, avoiding cephalosporins in patients with penicillin allergies likely developed from these early studies. [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Penicillin Allergy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cross-reactivity with other penicillin-related compounds, specifically cephalosporins, has resulted in the avoidance of drug use. Early studies may have also over-estimated cross-reactivity between penicillin and cephalosporins as the production of early generation cephalosporins may have been contaminated with small amounts of penicillin. Penicillin has a single R side chain whereas cephalosporins have two. If the penicillin side chain is similar to either cephalosporin side chain, there is a greater risk of cross-reactivity. This more commonly occurs with first and second generation cephalosporins than with third or fourth generation cephalosporins making the latter more attractive treatment choices when a patient has a proven penicillin allergy. Overall, cross-reactivity with aminopenicillins is less than 2%, carbapenems less than 1% and cephalosporins less than 3%. There is no significant reported cross-reactivity with monobactams.

retrieved_docs.title: Cefepime -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Cephalosporins are similar to penicillins with the beta-lactam ring but differ by side chains. The similarities of these side chains to the penicillin structure account for the IgE-mediated cross-reactivity and not the beta-lactam ring, as previously thought. The newer generation of cephalosporins has a greater difference in the side chain structures. A severe IgE-mediated hypersensitivity reaction to cefepime in a penicillin-allergic patient is rare. Delayed T-cell hypersensitivity reactions can still occur because T-cells can recognize the entire beta-lactam ring and the side chains. [31] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Cephalosporins -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Hypersensitivity reactions: A hypersensitivity reaction to cephalosporin is infrequent and is more common in first and second-generation cephalosporins. Common allergic reactions to cephalosporin include rash, hives, and swelling. The hypersensitivity reaction rarely results in anaphylaxis. Patients allergic to penicillin might also show a hypersensitive reaction to cephalosporins. This cross-reactivity is more common in first- and second-generation cephalosporins because they have R-groups more similar to penicillin G. Third-generation and beyond cephalosporins show minimal cross-reactivity. [39] [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Penicillin Allergy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Acute urticaria Allergic contact dermatitis Chronic urticaria Erythema multiforme Erythema nodosum Erythroderma Hypersensitivity vasculitis Irritant contact dermatitis Lichen planus Sweet syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Amoxicillin -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Understanding contraindications and nuances related to amoxicillin use is crucial for healthcare professionals. This overview explores contraindications stemming from previous anaphylactic reactions, considerations of crossover sensitivity, and the differentiation between type-I and type-IV hypersensitivity reactions in patients, offering insights into skin testing as a diagnostic tool. Prior occurrences of anaphylactic reactions or severe skin responses, such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome, to amoxicillin or other beta-lactams are a contraindication to amoxicillin. [38] These reactions may have crossover sensitivity with cephalosporins or carbapenems. Recent data suggests significantly lower cross-reactivity with these agents than once suspected.

retrieved_docs.title: Cefotaxime -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Hypersensitivity to cefotaxime is an absolute contraindication to its use. Patients with known allergies to penicillin or other cephalosporins should also avoid cefotaxime.

retrieved_docs.title: Penicillin Allergy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Penicillin is a beta-lactam antibiotic. Included in the penicillin family of antibiotics are penicillin G, penicillin V, procaine penicillin, benzathine penicillin as well as over 15 related compounds with broader antibacterial effects. Type 1 and type 4 hypersensitivity reactions mediate the most common allergies to penicillin. Serious allergic reactions to penicillin are Type 1 and mediated by IgE. Risk factors for IgE mediated reactions include high-dose parenteral administration and repetitive or frequent dosing of penicillins. Most severe reactions occur between the ages of 20 to 49 years. Previously, PCN related antibiotics such as cephalosporins were avoided given concern for cross-reactivity. Current research suggests that similarities between the chemical structures of the R-side chains are what determines cross-reactivity between classes of penicillin-related compounds, enabling greater use of antibiotics previously avoided. Of note, amoxicillin is the most common of the penicillins associated with cross-reactivity. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Penicillin Allergy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Penicillin (PCN) was discovered by Alexander Flemming in 1928 and became widely used in 1942 to treat various staphylococcal and streptococcal bacterial infections. Today, penicillin is still one of the most widely prescribed antibiotics. Penicillin is also one of the most frequently reported medication allergies. The penicillin family of antibiotics includes penicillin derivatives of ampicillin and amoxicillin as well as cephalosporins, monobactams, carbapenems, and beta-lactamase inhibitors. All penicillins share the same core beta-lactam and thiazolidine ring structures but differ in their side chains. Identification of true penicillin allergy is critical as 80% to 90% of people once considered allergic are ultimately able to tolerate penicillins resulting in the decreased need for broad-spectrum antibiotics and development of multi-drug resistant organisms. This activity reviews the pathophysiology of penicillin allergy and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the management of these patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Benzathine Penicillin -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Benzathine penicillin is contraindicated in patients with previous anaphylactic or severe skin reactions to penicillin (eg, Steven-Johnson or toxic epidermal necrosis). There have been reports of patients with anaphylactic or severe skin reactions from cephalosporins or carbapenems having a type of cross-reactivity reaction that also results in serious adverse events when they are administered any penicillin antimicrobial, including benzathine penicillin, but these reports are believed to be much less prevalent than previously suspected.

retrieved_docs.title: Amoxicillin Clavulanate -- Contraindications -- Hypersensitivity Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: The administration of amoxicillin to patients with a history of hypersensitivity reactions to amoxicillin, clavulanic acid, or other β-lactam antimicrobials is contraindicated. As amoxicillin is a penicillin derivative, there is a high likelihood of crossover hypersensitivity in patients with a documented penicillin allergy. In this population, clinicians should consider alternative antibiotics. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Penicillin Allergy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Penicillin (PCN) was discovered by Alexander Flemming in 1928 and became widely used in 1942 to treat various staphylococcal and streptococcal bacterial infections. Today, penicillin is still one of the most widely prescribed antibiotics. Penicillin is also one of the most frequently reported medication allergies. The penicillin family of antibiotics includes penicillin derivatives of ampicillin and amoxicillin as well as cephalosporins, monobactams, carbapenems, and beta-lactamase inhibitors. All penicillins share the same core beta-lactam and thiazolidine ring structures but differ in their side chains. Identification of true penicillin allergy is critical as 80% to 90% of people once considered allergic are ultimately able to tolerate penicillins resulting in the decreased need for broad-spectrum antibiotics and development of multi-drug resistant organisms. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Neurosyphilis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Penicillin remains the mainstay of treatment. Penicillin-allergic patients should be considered for confirmatory skin testing as most will be proven not to have a true allergy. Benzathine penicillin G is recommended for most types of syphilis, but neurosyphilis should be treated with aqueous crystalline penicillin G according to the CDC guidelines. Ceftriaxone is an acceptable alternative for penicillin-allergic patients who are unable or unwilling to undergo desensitization. Cross-reactivity is very low, penetration into the CSF is good, and the bacteria are sensitive even at low minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) levels. [29] [178] [179] [180] [181] [182] [183] After treatment, confirmatory CSF nontreponemal studies (VDRL) should be performed every 6 months to track disease until resolution. Retreatment should be considered if the CSF remains positive after 2 years.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurosyphilis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If penicillin desensitization is not possible or reasonable , ceftriaxone is recommended as an alternative therapy, as it penetrates the blood-brain barrier well and is bactericidal for T pallidum at low concentrations. [29] [178] [179] [180] [181] [182] [183] [184] [185] Allergic cross-reactivity with penicillin is very low, and ceftriaxone skin testing can be done for confirmation of safety in extremely allergic, high-risk individuals. [186]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 25
retrieved_docs.content: reaction,” and broader categories such as “drugs, medicaments and biological substances causing adverse effects in therapeutic use.” Amoxicillin and its formulations, as well as certain ampicillin preparations, are expressly contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to the β‑lactam penicillin class due to cross‑reactivity. Therapeutically, these agents are employed for both prophylaxis and treatment of bacterial infections at diverse sites. Penicillin G and ampicillin are noted for prophylaxis of unspecified bacterial infections; amoxicillin is used to prevent and treat bacterial infections, otitis media (middle‑ear infection), and polymicrobial human bite wounds. In the context of human bite injuries, amoxicillin, cefalexin, cefazolin, and cefotetan are identified as appropriate antimicrobial options. β‑lactam antibiotics—including benzathine‑penicillin G, carbenicillin indanyl sodium, dikloxacillin, the potassium salts of penicillin G and penicillin V, the disodium salts of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: The beta‑lactam antibacterial group classified as penicillins includes a wide range of specific agents—carbenicillin indanyl sodium, dikloxacilin, draselná sůl penicilinu G, dvojsodná sůl tikarcilinu, nafcillin sodium monohydrate, nafcillin sodný, oxacilin, penicilin V, piperacilin, sodná sůl dikloxacilinu, sodná sůl penicilinu G, and sodná sůl piperacilinu. All of these compounds share the characteristic β‑lactam ring and penicillin core that define their structural class. Because of this common β‑lactam structure, the penicillin class is contraindicated for use with trihydrát ampicilinu, reflecting the risk of cross‑reactivity or adverse interaction. Recognizing that these diverse penicillin derivatives belong to the same structural family helps clinicians anticipate similar antimicrobial spectra, resistance patterns, and potential allergic reactions, thereby guiding appropriate drug selection and avoidance of contraindicated combinations in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 112
retrieved_docs.content: delineate the therapeutic roles, prophylactic applications, structural taxonomy, contraindications, and safety considerations of ciprofloxacin and the extensive β‑lactam penicillin family, guiding clinicians in drug selection, adverse‑effect anticipation, and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 26
retrieved_docs.content: delineate the structural diversity, contraindications, prophylactic and therapeutic applications of penicillin‑type antibiotics, and the associated laboratory and immunological findings essential for informed clinical decision‑making and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314
retrieved_docs.content: Doripenem is classified within the carbapenem structural class, and biapenem is identified as a member of the same carbapenem class. Both agents belong to the carbapenem family of β‑lactam antibiotics, which are characterized by a broad spectrum of activity against Gram‑positive and Gram‑negative bacteria and a high resistance to most β‑lactamases. Their shared classification underscores their similar mechanisms of action—binding to penicillin‑binding proteins to inhibit cell‑wall synthesis—and their common clinical use in treating severe or resistant bacterial infections. Recognizing doripenem and biapenem as carbapenems informs clinicians about their pharmacologic properties, potential cross‑resistance, and appropriate therapeutic contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 106
retrieved_docs.content: these relationships delineate therapeutic uses, prophylactic roles, structural classifications, contraindications and safety considerations that guide clinical decision‑making for ciprofloxacin, penicillin‑derived antibiotics and related β‑lactam agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2050
retrieved_docs.content: The penicillin‑class antibacterial [EPC] is classified within the structural class of beta‑lactam antibacterials, penicillins. This beta‑lactam penicillin class encompasses a broad spectrum of agents, including dikloxacilin, nafcillin, nafcillin sodium monohydrate, nafcillin sodný, oxacilin, piperacilin, prokain penicilin G, sodná sůl oxacilinu, sodná sůl penicilinu G, and tikarcilin. Ampicilin is identified as a contraindicated member of the beta‑lactam antibacterials, penicillins, and the sodium salt of ampicillin (sodná sůl ampicilinu) is likewise contraindicated within this class. Consequently, while many drugs share the penicillin β‑lactam backbone, ampicilin and its sodium salt should be avoided in situations where the beta‑lactam penicillin class is indicated, reflecting concerns such as allergy or cross‑reactivity. Recognizing these structural relationships is essential for appropriate antimicrobial selection and for preventing the use of contraindicated agents in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2695
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotics encompass a range of specific antimicrobial agents, including cefalexin, cefotetan, and ertapenem, each representing a distinct member of the broader antibiotic category. Cefalexin is a first‑generation cephalosporin, cefotetan is a second‑generation cephalosporin, and ertapenem belongs to the carbapenem class; all are employed to treat bacterial infections. Recognizing that these drugs are individual children of the antibiotic family aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy based on the infection’s spectrum, resistance patterns, and pharmacologic properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 68
retrieved_docs.content: V; management of human‑bite injuries with cefalexin, cefazolin, cefotetan, ampicillin and amoxicillin—human‑bite injuries being classified as a child event of “Other and unspecified environmental and accidental causes” within the broader “Other specified and classifiable” e‑code category that also contains radiation exposure; and treatment of prostatitis with carbenicillin, carbenicillin indanyl sodium and double‑sodium salt of carbenicillin. The network integrates adverse‑effect coding (e‑codes), recording allergic reactions to penicillin and general beta‑lactam hypersensitivity as narrower manifestations, with antielminthica noted as a related subset and beta‑laktamy identified as a cause of adverse reactions. This hierarchical and relational structure supports accurate documentation of drug classes, salt variants, contraindications, infection classifications, and side‑effect monitoring to guide antimicrobial stewardship and patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2025
retrieved_docs.content: Penicillin‑class Antibacterial [EPC] is classified within the structural class of beta‑lactam antibacterials, penicillins. Within this class, amdinocilin is identified as a child entity, and benzathin‑penicilin G is also a member. Additional agents that belong to the beta‑lactam, penicillin structural group include dikloxacilin, dvojsodná sůl tikarcilinu, nafcilin, nafcillin sodium monohydrate, nafcillin sodný, penicilin V, sodná sůl dikloxacilinu, sodná sůl piperacilinu, and tikarcilin. All of these compounds share the characteristic beta‑lactam ring that confers antibacterial activity, and recognizing their common structural classification helps clinicians anticipate similar spectra of activity, potential cross‑allergy, and pharmacologic considerations when selecting penicillin‑based therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 529
retrieved_docs.content: Second generation cephalosporins constitute a subclass of β‑lactam antibiotics that share a common chemical backbone and antimicrobial spectrum. Within this subclass, cefuroxim, cefaklor and cefprozil are identified as child compounds, indicating they are specific agents derived from the second‑generation cephalosporin core. In addition, cefaklor bezvodý is classified as a type of second generation cephalosporin, further illustrating the range of formulations encompassed by this group. Recognizing that cefuroxim, cefaklor, cefaklor bezvodý and cefprozil all belong to the second generation cephalosporin class assists clinicians in predicting their antibacterial activity, pharmacokinetic behavior, and potential cross‑reactivity when selecting appropriate antimicrobial therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1631
retrieved_docs.content: Aztreonam is classified within the monobactam structural class, indicating that its chemical framework belongs to this group of β‑lactam antibiotics. Both cefmetazol and cilastatin are described as child compounds of the monobactam class, suggesting that they share core structural features or pharmacologic characteristics with monobactam agents. In addition, the monobactam class is identified as a type of tazobactam, linking these agents to the broader family of β‑lactamase inhibitor–containing compounds. Together, these relationships highlight the interconnected taxonomy of aztreonam, cefmetazol, cilastatin, monobactam, and tazobactam, which is important for understanding their mechanisms of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and considerations in antimicrobial therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: indicating that their use is discouraged in patients for whom penicillin therapy is indicated. Overall, penicillin‑derived agents serve both prophylactic and therapeutic roles against bacterial pathogens in the eye, ear, and male genital tract, while requiring careful consideration of contraindications and potential β‑lactam–related adverse reactions for safe prescribing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1283
retrieved_docs.content: Penicillin‑class Antibacterial [EPC] and specific agents such as benzathin‑penicilin G, dikloxacilin, draselná sůl penicilinu G (sodium salt of penicillin G), nafcillin sodný (nafcillin sodium), piperacilin, sodná sůl dikloxacilinu (sodium salt of dikloxacillin), sodná sůl piperacilinu (sodium salt of piperacillin), and tikarcilin are all members of the structural class “beta‑lactam antibacterials, penicillins.” This classification indicates that each of these compounds contains the characteristic beta‑lactam ring of penicillin‑type antibiotics, sharing similar mechanisms of action, antimicrobial spectra, and potential for cross‑reactivity. Recognizing these agents as part of the same beta‑lactam penicillin structural class aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy, anticipating resistance patterns, and managing allergy considerations across related drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: as **tikarcilin**. Certain formulations are flagged as **contraindicated_class_of** the beta‑lactam penicillin class, indicating they should be avoided in patients with β‑lactam hypersensitivity. The contraindicated entities are **amoxicilin**, its sodium salt (**amoxicilin sodná sůl**), **amoxicilin trihydrát**, **amoxicillin anhydrous**, **ampicilin**, the sodium salt of **ampicilin**, and the trihydrate form of **ampicilin**. Therapeutic contexts are explicitly linked to antimicrobial categories. **Antibiotics, Gynecological** includes **karfecilin**, a child of **antipseudomonal penicillin**, and is cross‑referenced to **Antifungal Antibiotics, Topical**, reflecting overlap between gynecological antibacterial and topical antifungal treatments. **Ampicilin** is noted for both **preventive activity against bacterial infection** and for **treating human‑bite injuries (عضة إنسان)**; human bite is classified under the “Other and unspecified environmental and accidental causes” branch

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 399
retrieved_docs.content: The beta‑lactam antibacterials, penicillins, form a structural class defined by a β‑lactam ring and a penicillin core, conferring a common mechanism of bacterial cell‑wall synthesis inhibition and a potential for cross‑reactivity. Members of this class include Penicillin‑class Antibacterial [EPC], Penem Antibacterial [EPC], amdinocilin, benzathin‑penicilin G, cyklacilin, dikloxacilin, draselná sůl penicilinu G, dvojsodná sůl tikarcilinu, nafcilin, nafcillin sodium monohydrate, nafcillin sodný, oxacilin, penicilin V, piperacilin, prokain penicilin G, sodium salts of dikloxacilin, oxacilin, penicilin G, piperacilin, and tikarcilin. All of these agents are indicated to may_prevent bacterial infections and may be used prophylactically to reduce infection risk, with a positive bacterial culture serving as a diagnostic confirmation of infection. A specific contraindication exists for co‑administration of any beta‑lactam penicillin with the sodium salt of ampicillin or with ampicillin trihydrate, as such combinations can produce adverse drug interactions. Recognizing the shared structural classification, common pharmacologic action, and the ampicillin‑related contraindication aids clinicians in selecting appropriate antibacterial therapy while minimizing allergic and interaction risks.
